title: My Dog Ate an Avocado Pit and Skin: Triage and Next Steps
meta_desc: If your dog ate an avocado pit or skin, read this guide on toxicity risks, choking hazards, and when to seek emergency veterinary care immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: While avocado flesh is mildly toxic, the skin and pit pose significant risks for gastrointestinal upset and life-threatening physical obstructions. Immediate veterinary attention is required if a dog swallows an avocado pit, as it often necessitates surgical removal.

What are the potential health risks if my dog eats an avocado?

  • Avocados contain a fungicidal toxin called persin, which is most concentrated in the skin and pit.
  • While the fruit's flesh causes mild issues, the skin can lead to significant gastrointestinal upset.
  • The pit is the greatest danger, presenting a high risk of physical obstruction in the digestive tract.

Is it a medical emergency if my dog swallowed an avocado pit?

  • This is a Medium Urgency situation because the pit is a major hazard that cannot be digested.
  • Since the pit is large and round, it cannot be broken down by stomach acid.
  • It can easily become lodged in the esophagus or small intestine, potentially requiring surgical intervention to prevent life-threatening complications.

How does providing a photo help my veterinarian triage the situation?

  • Providing a photo of the avocado variety or a similar pit helps the vet estimate the size of the object consumed.
  • Knowing the texture and size allows the medical team to predict if the pit will pass naturally.
  • This helps the vet determine the likelihood of a foreign body obstruction based on your dog's specific size and weight.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

While dogs appear relatively resistant to avocado toxicosis compared to other species, ingestion of the fruit, leaves, stems, and seeds can be associated with adverse effects. The toxic principle, persin, can cause myocardial necrosis in sufficient dosages. Clinical signs in dogs may include weakness, depression, vomiting, ataxia, tremors, and/or hyperthermia. Diagnosis is based on a history of exposure and observed clinical signs. Differential diagnoses should include ethylene glycol toxicosis, ingestion of hypotensive agents, and infectious diseases.

Chapter: Toxicology, Cardiology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 2965)

faqs.question: Watch for repeated vomiting, lethargy, loss of appetite, abdominal pain, or straining to defecate. If you see these signs, head to an emergency vet immediately.
Do not attempt to induce vomiting without a veterinarian's explicit instruction. If the pit is large, it can become stuck in the esophagus on the way back up, causing a secondary emergency.
The skin contains persin which can cause vomiting and diarrhea. While usually not fatal, it can lead to significant stomach distress and dehydration.
